description: Full Transformer model architecture diagram showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, and positional encoding, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, and feed-forward layers, connected by residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Gives students a complete visual map of how encoder and decoder components connect, making it easier to understand data flow, residual connections, and the role of each sub-layer before diving into individual components.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer lesson as an orientation map; return to it each time a new sub-component (attention, FFN, positional encoding) is introduced so students can locate it in the full pipeline.
suggested_caption: The Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Scaled Dot-Product Attention diagram showing the sequential operations: MatMul of Q and K, scaling by 1/sqrt(d_k), optional masking, softmax, then MatMul with V to produce output.
teaching_value: Visually decomposes the attention computation step-by-step, helping students trace exactly how queries, keys, and values interact and why the scaling factor is applied before softmax.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention formula; use it to walk through each arithmetic step before presenting the matrix equation, and again when explaining why scaling prevents vanishing gradients in softmax.
suggested_caption: Scaled Dot-Product Attention: Q·K scaled, softmaxed, then applied to V.

description: Multi-Head Attention diagram showing h parallel scaled dot-product attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear projection, with Q, K, V inputs each going through separate linear layers per head.
teaching_value: Illustrates why multi-head attention is more powerful than single-head: students can see how independent linear projections allow each head to attend to different representation subspaces simultaneously.
when_to_show: Show immediately after explaining single-head scaled dot-product attention; use it to motivate the multi-head design and explain the role of the linear layers before and after the parallel heads.
suggested_caption: Multi-Head Attention: h parallel attention heads concatenated and projected.

description: Attention visualization showing colored lines from the word 'making' in an encoder self-attention layer (layer 5 of 6) connecting to distant tokens, illustrating how the model captures the long-range dependency 'making…more difficult'. Different colors represent different attention heads.
teaching_value: Provides concrete empirical evidence that self-attention learns meaningful long-distance syntactic/semantic dependencies, helping students understand why attention outperforms RNNs for capturing long-range context.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the advantages of self-attention over recurrent models for capturing long-distance dependencies, or when motivating why the Transformer was designed without recurrence.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance dependency for 'making' (layer 5).

description: Top panel of anaphora resolution visualization: full attention pattern for head 5 in encoder layer 5 of 6, showing broad attention weights across all tokens in the sentence.
teaching_value: Shows the full attention distribution of a head involved in anaphora resolution, helping students understand how attention heads can specialize for coreference tasks and what 'full' attention patterns look like.
when_to_show: Show alongside the isolated 'its' attention panel (Image 6) to contrast full vs. focused attention patterns; use when teaching how different heads learn different linguistic functions.
suggested_caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 involved in anaphora resolution (layer 5).

description: Bottom panel of anaphora resolution visualization: isolated attention lines from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to the antecedent noun, demonstrating coreference resolution behavior.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that specific attention heads learn to resolve pronoun-antecedent relationships with high confidence (sharp attention), giving students a concrete example of emergent linguistic structure in learned attention.
when_to_show: Show after Image 5 to highlight the contrast between broad and sharp attention; use when explaining how attention heads can specialize and what 'sharp' attention distributions mean for model interpretability.
suggested_caption: Sharp attention from 'its' resolving anaphora in heads 5 and 6 (layer 5).

description: First of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing attention patterns that appear to reflect syntactic sentence structure, with one head attending in a pattern consistent with a specific grammatical relationship.
teaching_value: Provides evidence that attention heads implicitly learn syntactic structure without explicit supervision, helping students understand the representational power and interpretability of multi-head attention.
when_to_show: Show when discussing what attention heads learn and model interpretability; pair with Image 8 to illustrate that different heads learn complementary structural patterns.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence-structural patterns (layer 5, head A).

description: Second of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing a different attention pattern from the first head, demonstrating that distinct heads specialize in different structural or semantic tasks.
teaching_value: Reinforces the concept of head specialization by showing a contrasting pattern from a different head in the same layer, making the division of labor among heads tangible for students.
when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 7 as a direct comparison; use to reinforce why multiple heads are beneficial and to discuss how the model distributes different linguistic tasks across heads.
suggested_caption: A second encoder self-attention head learning different structural patterns (layer 5, head B).

alt: Math behind √dₖ Scaling Factor in Attention
description: A mathematical diagram illustrating the derivation and justification of the √dₖ scaling factor used in scaled dot-product attention, likely showing variance analysis of dot products as dimensionality dₖ grows and how dividing by √dₖ stabilizes the variance to 1.
teaching_value: Helps students understand WHY the scaling factor √dₖ is necessary — without it, dot products grow in magnitude with embedding dimension, pushing softmax into regions of near-zero gradients; the diagram makes the statistical reasoning (variance scaling) concrete and visual.
when_to_show: Show this after introducing the raw dot-product attention formula Q·Kᵀ and before presenting the full scaled attention formula softmax(Q·Kᵀ/√dₖ)·V — use it to motivate WHY scaling is needed, not just that it exists.
suggested_caption: Why √dₖ? Scaling prevents softmax saturation as dimension grows. (Outcome School)

description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data point clouds: original data (left), mean-subtracted/centered data (middle), and PCA-rotated data (right), illustrating the geometric effect of each preprocessing step.
teaching_value: Gives students an intuitive geometric understanding of how mean subtraction centers data at the origin and how PCA rotates the coordinate axes to align with directions of maximum variance, making abstract linear algebra operations concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing data preprocessing before training neural networks, specifically when explaining why centering data matters and what PCA does geometrically.
suggested_caption: Data preprocessing steps: raw, mean-centered, and PCA-rotated. (cs231n)

description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data clouds: PCA-rotated data (left), whitened data with equal variance in all dimensions (middle), and a noisy whitened version (right), illustrating the effect of whitening and its noise-amplification weakness.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize how whitening normalizes variance across all dimensions to produce an identity covariance, and concretely demonstrates the risk of noise amplification when small-variance (noisy) dimensions are stretched equally.
when_to_show: Show after explaining PCA, when introducing whitening as the next preprocessing step, and when discussing its trade-offs including noise sensitivity.
suggested_caption: Whitening normalizes variance per dimension; right panel shows noise amplification. (cs231n)

description: Visualization of PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches: showing original image patches, their PCA components (eigenvectors as images), and the resulting whitened patches, demonstrating the transformation on real image data.
teaching_value: Bridges the abstract 2D geometric intuition to real high-dimensional image data, showing students what PCA eigenvectors look like as images and how whitening affects actual pixel patches, making the concepts tangible for computer vision applications.
when_to_show: Show when students ask how PCA/whitening applies to real image data, or after the abstract 2D visualizations to ground the concepts in a practical computer vision context.
suggested_caption: PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches. (cs231n)

description: Side-by-side diagram of a neural network without dropout (left, all connections active) versus with dropout (right, some neurons and their connections randomly removed/crossed out), illustrating the dropout regularization technique.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the core mechanism of dropout — randomly deactivating neurons during training — helping students understand how it prevents co-adaptation of neurons and acts as an ensemble method, which is otherwise difficult to grasp from equations alone.
when_to_show: Show when introducing dropout as a regularization technique, especially after covering L1/L2 regularization, to contrast structural regularization approaches with weight-penalty approaches.
suggested_caption: Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training to reduce overfitting. (cs231n)

description: A 2D scatter plot showing two classes of data points arranged as two curves on a plane, color-coded to distinguish the two classes
teaching_value: Establishes the baseline classification problem — students see the raw input data before any network transformation, making it concrete what the network must learn to separate
when_to_show: At the very start of introducing neural network classification; use as the 'problem statement' before showing any network architecture or transformation
suggested_caption: Raw 2D dataset: two curves the network must classify. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 2D visualization showing how a neural network with hidden layers classifies every point in the input space, displaying a non-linear decision boundary separating the two curve classes
teaching_value: Contrasts with linear separation to show that hidden layers enable non-linear decision boundaries; students can directly compare what a deeper network achieves versus a simple linear classifier
when_to_show: After showing the linear classifier result; use to motivate why hidden layers matter by visually demonstrating the gain in expressive power
suggested_caption: Hidden layers enable non-linear decision boundaries on the same dataset. (Olah, 2014)

description: A diagram of a small neural network architecture showing input layer, one or more hidden layers, and output layer with labeled nodes and connections
teaching_value: Provides a concrete architectural reference so students can map abstract layer concepts (input, hidden, output) to a specific network structure used in the examples
when_to_show: When first introducing the network structure being analyzed; reference back to it whenever discussing what happens 'at each layer' during the transformation visualizations
suggested_caption: Example neural network architecture with input, hidden, and output layers. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 2D visualization of the dataset as seen in the input (raw) representation — the original space before any layer transformation — showing the two curve classes
teaching_value: Anchors the concept of 'representation at a layer' by showing the data in its unmodified input form, giving students a baseline to compare against transformed representations
when_to_show: When introducing the idea that each layer creates a new representation; show this as 'Layer 0' before revealing what the hidden layer does to the same data
suggested_caption: Data in its raw input representation before any network transformation. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 2D visualization of the same dataset after transformation by the first hidden layer, showing how the data distribution has changed shape in the new representation space
teaching_value: Directly demonstrates that hidden layers reshape data into new representations; comparing this to the input representation makes the concept of learned transformations tangible
when_to_show: Immediately after showing the input-space visualization (Image 4); ask students to identify what changed and why that change helps with linear separability
suggested_caption: Same data after first hidden layer transformation — a new learned representation. (Olah, 2014)

alt: Gradually applying a neural network layer
description: An animated GIF showing the continuous transformation of 2D data as a tanh neural network layer is gradually applied, visualizing the smooth deformation of the data manifold
teaching_value: Makes the abstract idea of a layer as a continuous homeomorphism concrete; students see that the transformation is smooth and invertible, connecting neural networks to topology
when_to_show: When explaining how a single layer transforms data; use to bridge from the discrete layer-by-layer view to the topological interpretation of network layers
suggested_caption: Continuous application of a tanh layer gradually deforms the data manifold. (Olah, 2014)

description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network attempting to classify two slightly entangled spirals, visualizing the progressive transformation through hidden layers until the spirals become linearly separable
teaching_value: Shows a successful case of deep network classification through topology — students see how multiple layers cooperate to untangle a complex manifold that no single layer could handle
when_to_show: After explaining the continuous layer transformation concept; use as a success case before contrasting with the failure case on more entangled spirals
suggested_caption: Four hidden layers successfully untangle slightly entangled spirals into linearly separable classes. (Olah, 2014)

description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network failing to classify two more heavily entangled spirals, with the network unable to achieve linear separability despite multiple layers
teaching_value: Illustrates the topological limits of low-dimensional networks — students see that network depth alone cannot overcome topological constraints when the network is too narrow
when_to_show: Directly after the successful spiral case (Image 7); the contrast drives home that topology imposes hard limits, motivating the formal lower-bound argument on network width
suggested_caption: A low-dimensional network fails to untangle more entangled spirals — topology wins. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 2D plot showing the topology-based classification problem: class A as a filled disk centered at the origin and class B as an annular ring surrounding it, illustrating a topologically non-trivial dataset
teaching_value: Provides the concrete geometric setup for the topological impossibility proof; students can visually grasp why no continuous deformation with only 2 hidden units can separate a surrounded region from its surroundings
when_to_show: When introducing the formal topology and classification section; use to make the abstract set definitions A={x|d(x,0)<1/3} and B concrete before walking through the proof
suggested_caption: Topologically challenging dataset: class A surrounded by class B in 2D. (Olah, 2014)

description: An animated GIF showing a neural network with only two hidden units attempting and failing to classify the surrounded-disk dataset, getting stuck in an unproductive local minimum
teaching_value: Empirically validates the theoretical impossibility claim — students see the network struggling and converging to ~80% accuracy, reinforcing that the failure is structural/topological, not just a training issue
when_to_show: After presenting the impossibility proof; use as empirical confirmation that the theoretical lower bound on hidden units is real and observable in practice
suggested_caption: Two hidden units cannot topologically separate the surrounded dataset — stuck at ~80%. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 3D visualization showing two classes of data points (A and B) where one class surrounds the other in 3D space, illustrating a topological classification problem that cannot be solved by a hyperplane in lower dimensions.
teaching_value: Demonstrates visually why topological entanglement (one class surrounding another) makes linear separation impossible without adding dimensions, grounding the abstract proof about homeomorphisms and determinants.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the proof that standard layers fail for topologically entangled datasets; use to make the abstract argument about determinants and homeomorphisms concrete.
suggested_caption: 3D dataset where class A is surrounded by B, preventing hyperplane separation. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 1D-to-2D transformation visualization showing how a third hidden unit maps the 1D dataset (intervals A and B on a line) into a 2D representation where the two classes become linearly separable.
teaching_value: Shows concretely how adding a hidden unit lifts a topologically inseparable 1D problem into a higher-dimensional space where a hyperplane can separate the classes, making the role of network width intuitive.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the 1D classification problem (A = [-1/3, 1/3], B = two outer intervals) to illustrate how a third hidden unit resolves the topological obstruction.
suggested_caption: Third hidden unit lifts 1D entangled classes into linearly separable 2D space. (Olah, 2014)

description: An animated GIF showing the training process of a neural network transforming a 1D dataset into a 2D representation, visualizing how the network learns to map the input intervals A and B into a separable configuration.
teaching_value: Animates the learning dynamics of how hidden units progressively reshape the data manifold during training, making the abstract concept of learned representations tangible and showing the process rather than just the result.
when_to_show: Show during discussion of how individual hidden units learn threshold functions (firing when x > -1/2 or x > 1/2); use to connect the mathematical description to the visual learning process.
suggested_caption: Animation of neural network learning to separate 1D topological classes during training. (Olah, 2014)

description: A visualization of two linked rings (a Hopf link or similar link) in 3D space, representing two interlinked 1D manifolds (classes A and B) that cannot be separated without a 4th dimension.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete geometric example of topological entanglement beyond simple containment, showing students that linked manifolds pose classification challenges analogous to the surrounding-class problem but in a more realistic setting.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from the 'one class surrounds another' scenario to more general topological obstructions; use to motivate why neural networks need sufficient dimensionality to untangle manifolds.
suggested_caption: Two linked rings: a topological obstruction requiring a 4th dimension to separate. (Olah, 2014)

description: A side-by-side or comparative visualization showing a linked pair of tori (topologically entangled) alongside an unlinked configuration, illustrating the concept of an unlink and the role of neural network layers as ambient isotopies.
teaching_value: Connects the abstract topological concept of links/unlinks to neural network classification, showing that a network's continuous transformation of representations is mathematically equivalent to an ambient isotopy that untangles the manifolds.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how neural network layer transformations correspond to ambient isotopies; use to explain why 3 hidden units suffice to classify an unlink but not a true link.
suggested_caption: Linked vs. unlinked tori: neural networks untangle manifolds via ambient isotopy. (Olah, 2014)

description: A visualization of a tangled or knotted manifold, illustrating the geometric complexity that arises when a neural network attempts to separate entangled data manifolds, potentially showing high-derivative regions or near-discontinuities.
teaching_value: Illustrates the 'easy way out' problem where a network might create sharp, high-derivative deformations instead of smooth untangling, motivating the use of contractive penalties to enforce smooth representations.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the failure modes of neural networks on topologically complex data and the motivation for contractive regularization; connects the 2n+2 dimension theorem to practical training concerns.
suggested_caption: Tangled manifold illustrating why smooth untangling requires contractive regularization. (Olah, 2014)

description: A grid deformation visualization showing a vector field applied to a 2D grid, where arrows indicate the direction and magnitude of spatial deformation, illustrating how a learned vector field can reshape the data manifold.
teaching_value: Makes the proposed alternative layer type concrete by showing how a vector field deforms space, helping students understand the geometric intuition behind manifold-aware layers as an alternative to standard affine+activation layers.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the proposed vector-field-based layer architecture; use to contrast with standard affine transformations and illustrate how anchor-point interpolation could enable more flexible manifold manipulation.
suggested_caption: Vector field deforming a grid, illustrating a proposed manifold-aware neural network layer. (Olah, 2014)

description: A 'bubble' deformation of a grid showing a localized, radially symmetric vector field that pushes or pulls a region of space outward, corresponding to the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)).
teaching_value: Provides a specific visual example of the proposed vector field layer formula, showing how anchor points create localized deformations that could be learned to separate manifolds, making the mathematical formula geometrically interpretable.
when_to_show: Show alongside the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)) to help students connect the mathematical expression to its geometric effect on the data space.
suggested_caption: Bubble deformation from a two-anchor vector field layer formula. (Olah, 2014)
